subject: maths
title: Introduction to Trigonometry

nodes.title: Define sin, cos, tan from a right triangle
nodes.brief: State the three primary trigonometric ratios of an acute angle as ratios of the opposite side, adjacent side, and hypotenuse of a right triangle.
nodes.quote: sine of angle A = side opposite to angle A / hypotenuse = BC / AC
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The right triangle and its sides.
In right triangle ABC right-angled at B, angle A is one of the acute angles. The side BC is opposite angle A (it faces A), AB is adjacent to angle A (it forms the angle along with the hypotenuse), and AC is the hypotenuse (the side opposite the right angle, always the longest side). Example: if A is the bottom-left vertex and C is the top vertex, the hypotenuse is AC.

PART 2 — Defining sin, cos, tan.
The three primary ratios are: sin A = BC/AC (opposite over hypotenuse), cos A = AB/AC (adjacent over hypotenuse), tan A = BC/AB (opposite over adjacent). A useful mnemonic is SOH-CAH-TOA: Sin = Opposite/Hypotenuse, Cos = Adjacent/Hypotenuse, Tan = Opposite/Adjacent.

PART 3 — Why the ratios depend only on the angle, not the triangle size.
All right triangles with the same acute angle A are similar (AA similarity). Similar triangles have proportional sides, so the ratio opposite/hypotenuse is the same regardless of how large or small the triangle is. This means sin A, cos A, and tan A are fixed by angle A alone.
keyMoves: Identify which side is opposite the angle, which is adjacent, and which is the hypotenuse
Write sin = opposite/hypotenuse, cos = adjacent/hypotenuse, tan = opposite/adjacent
Use the correct triangle vertices to read off the side lengths
misconceptions: Confusing the opposite and adjacent sides relative to the angle
Treating the hypotenuse as a leg
Believing the ratio changes if the triangle is scaled

nodes.title: Define cosec, sec, cot as reciprocals
nodes.brief: Define the reciprocal trigonometric ratios cosec, sec, and cot as the reciprocals of sin, cos, and tan respectively.
nodes.quote: cosecant of angle A = 1 / sine of angle A = hypotenuse / side opposite to angle A = AC / BC
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The three reciprocal ratios.
For any acute angle A in a right triangle, we define three more ratios as reciprocals of the primary ones: cosec A = 1/sin A = AC/BC (hypotenuse over opposite), sec A = 1/cos A = AC/AB (hypotenuse over adjacent), and cot A = 1/tan A = AB/BC (adjacent over opposite). Each reciprocal pair multiplies to 1: sin A * cosec A = 1, and so on.

PART 2 — Pairing them correctly.
The critical pairing rule is: cosec pairs with sin, sec pairs with cos, cot pairs with tan. A common error is swapping sec and cosec. Remember: sec starts with 'se' matching the 'co' in cosine — sec A = 1/cos A. Example: in triangle ABC with BC = 3, AB = 4, AC = 5, sin A = 3/5 so cosec A = 5/3; cos A = 4/5 so sec A = 5/4; tan A = 3/4 so cot A = 4/3.

PART 3 — Using reciprocals to switch forms.
If you know sin A = 3/5, you immediately have cosec A = 5/3 without any new triangle computation. This lets you write six ratios from a single ratio by combining the reciprocal rule with the Pythagoras theorem to find the missing side.
keyMoves: Pair each reciprocal ratio with its primary ratio (cosec with sin, sec with cos, cot with tan)
Invert the side ratio to get the reciprocal ratio
Recognise sec pairs with cos (not sin) — a common slip
misconceptions: Pairing sec with sin and cosec with cos
Reading 'cos A' as the abbreviation for cosecant of A
Thinking cot A means cot times A

nodes.title: Express tan and cot via sin and cos
nodes.brief: Use the quotient relations tan A = sin A/cos A and cot A = cos A/sin A to rewrite ratios in terms of sine and cosine.
nodes.quote: tan A = BC/AB = (BC/AC)/(AB/AC) = sin A / cos A and cot A = cos A / sin A
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Deriving tan A = sin A / cos A.
From the definitions: sin A = BC/AC and cos A = AB/AC. Dividing sin A by cos A: (BC/AC) / (AB/AC) = BC/AB = tan A. So tan A = sin A / cos A. This is the quotient identity for tangent.

PART 2 — The quotient identity for cotangent.
Similarly, cot A = AB/BC = (AB/AC)/(BC/AC) = cos A / sin A. Note that cot A = 1/tan A confirms this: 1/(sin A/cos A) = cos A/sin A. Example: if sin A = 4/5 and cos A = 3/5, then tan A = (4/5)/(3/5) = 4/3 and cot A = (3/5)/(4/5) = 3/4.

PART 3 — Why this matters in proofs.
Converting everything to sin and cos via tan A = sin A/cos A and cot A = cos A/sin A is the standard opening move for simplifying complicated trig expressions. Once all ratios are in sin and cos, common factors cancel cleanly and Pythagorean identities apply directly.
keyMoves: Write tan as sin over cos and cot as cos over sin
Use the relation to convert mixed expressions to sin/cos form
Recognise tan and cot are reciprocals of each other
misconceptions: Writing tan A = cos A/sin A
Forgetting cos A cannot be zero for tan to be defined
Mixing up which ratio goes on top

nodes.title: Find all ratios given one ratio
nodes.brief: Given one trigonometric ratio of an acute angle, construct the triangle and determine all the remaining ratios.
nodes.quote: Given tan A = 4/3, find the other trigonometric ratios of the angle A.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Set up the triangle from the given ratio.
tan A = opposite/adjacent = 4/3, so assign opposite = 4 and adjacent = 3 (any multiple works, pick the simplest integers). Use the Pythagoras theorem: hypotenuse^2 = 4^2 + 3^2 = 16 + 9 = 25, so hypotenuse = 5.

PART 2 — Read off all six ratios.
With opposite = 4, adjacent = 3, hypotenuse = 5: sin A = 4/5, cos A = 3/5, tan A = 4/3 (as given). The reciprocals are cosec A = 5/4, sec A = 5/3, cot A = 3/4. Double-check: sin^2 A + cos^2 A = 16/25 + 9/25 = 25/25 = 1. Correct.

PART 3 — Why any one ratio fixes all the others.
All right triangles with the same acute angle A are similar, so their side ratios are fixed. Knowing one ratio (like tan A = 4/3) pins the shape of the triangle, which pins every other ratio. The hypotenuse is always found by Pythagoras from the other two sides.
keyMoves: Assign opposite and adjacent sides from the given ratio
Use Pythagoras to find the missing side (here the hypotenuse = 5)
Read off every remaining ratio from the completed triangle
misconceptions: Forgetting to apply Pythagoras to find the third side
Swapping opposite and adjacent when reading tan
Assuming the hypotenuse equals the sum of the legs

nodes.title: Trigonometric ratios of 45 degrees
nodes.brief: Recall the exact trigonometric ratios of 45 degrees from an isosceles right triangle.
nodes.quote: sin 45 = side opposite to angle 45 / hypotenuse = a / (a*sqrt(2)) = 1/sqrt(2)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The isosceles right triangle.
In a right triangle with both acute angles equal to 45 degrees, the two legs must be equal (let each = a). By the Pythagoras theorem, hypotenuse = sqrt(a^2 + a^2) = sqrt(2) * a.

PART 2 — Computing sin 45, cos 45, tan 45.
With opposite = adjacent = a and hypotenuse = sqrt(2) * a: sin 45 = a/(sqrt(2)*a) = 1/sqrt(2), cos 45 = a/(sqrt(2)*a) = 1/sqrt(2), tan 45 = a/a = 1. So sin 45 = cos 45 = 1/sqrt(2) and tan 45 = 1.

PART 3 — The reciprocal ratios at 45 degrees.
cosec 45 = 1/sin 45 = sqrt(2), sec 45 = 1/cos 45 = sqrt(2), cot 45 = 1/tan 45 = 1. All six ratios at 45 degrees come from this single isosceles right triangle.
keyMoves: Use the isosceles right triangle to see the legs are equal
Read tan 45 = 1 directly from equal legs
Get sin and cos as 1/sqrt(2) using the sqrt(2) hypotenuse
misconceptions: Confusing 1/sqrt(2) with sqrt(2)
Mixing the 45 degree values with the 30/60 degree values
Thinking tan 45 is anything other than 1

nodes.title: Trigonometric ratios of 30 and 60 degrees
nodes.brief: Recall the exact trigonometric ratios of 30 and 60 degrees from an equilateral triangle bisected by an altitude.
nodes.quote: sin 30 = BD/AB = a/(2a) = 1/2, cos 30 = AD/AB = (a*sqrt(3))/(2a) = sqrt(3)/2, tan 30 = BD/AD = a/(a*sqrt(3)) = 1/sqrt(3)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The 30-60-90 triangle from an equilateral triangle.
Take an equilateral triangle ABC with all sides = 2a. Drop the altitude AD from A to BC; it bisects the base so BD = a. By the Pythagoras theorem: AD^2 = AB^2 - BD^2 = (2a)^2 - a^2 = 3a^2, so AD = a*sqrt(3). Triangle ABD has angle ABD = 60 degrees and angle BAD = 30 degrees.

PART 2 — Ratios at 30 degrees.
In triangle ABD, for angle B = 60 degrees: opposite = AD = a*sqrt(3), adjacent = BD = a, hypotenuse = AB = 2a. For angle A = 30 degrees: opposite = BD = a, adjacent = AD = a*sqrt(3), hypotenuse = 2a. So sin 30 = 1/2, cos 30 = sqrt(3)/2, tan 30 = 1/sqrt(3).

PART 3 — Ratios at 60 degrees.
For angle B = 60 degrees in the same triangle: sin 60 = sqrt(3)/2, cos 60 = 1/2, tan 60 = sqrt(3). The pattern: the 30 degree and 60 degree sin and cos values swap. The reciprocals follow: cosec 30 = 2, sec 30 = 2/sqrt(3), cot 30 = sqrt(3); cosec 60 = 2/sqrt(3), sec 60 = 2, cot 60 = 1/sqrt(3).
keyMoves: Bisect an equilateral triangle to form a 30-60-90 triangle
Read sin 30 = 1/2 and cos 30 = sqrt(3)/2 from the side ratios
Swap opposite/adjacent to get the 60 degree values
misconceptions: Swapping the sin and cos values between 30 and 60 degrees
Writing tan 30 = sqrt(3) instead of 1/sqrt(3)
Confusing cosec and sec for these angles

refreshers.answer: An equilateral triangle is one whose three sides are all equal in length. Because equal sides force equal opposite angles, all three of its angles are equal too; since the angles of any triangle add up to 180 degrees, each angle is 180/3 = 60 degrees. An altitude (or height) is the straight line drawn from one vertex perpendicular - at 90 degrees - to the opposite side. In an equilateral triangle this altitude is special: it lands exactly on the midpoint of the base, splitting that side into two equal halves (it is also the perpendicular bisector and it bisects the 60-degree top angle into two 30-degree halves). So if the equilateral triangle has side 2a, the altitude cuts the base into two pieces of length a each, and creates a right triangle whose angles are 90 degrees (where the altitude meets the base), 60 degrees (the original base angle), and 30 degrees (half the top angle). That 30-60-90 right triangle is the tool used to read off the exact ratios for 30 and 60 degrees.
ladder: In an equilateral triangle, what is true about all three sides, and so about all three angles?
If all three angles are equal and add to 180 degrees, how big is each one?
When you drop a perpendicular from the top vertex to the base, into what two pieces does it cut the base, and why?
refreshers.trigger: Builds the 30-60-90 triangle from an equilateral triangle and its altitude, assuming both ideas are known
refreshers.returnCue: With the equilateral triangle halved into a 30-60-90 right triangle, we can compute the ratios.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: This construction starts from an equilateral triangle and drops an altitude that bisects the base - what do equilateral and that altitude actually give us?

refreshers.trigger: Finds the altitude AD by computing AD^2 = AB^2 - BD^2 = 3a^2, assuming the Pythagoras theorem is known
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: Quick one before we read off the ratios: once you know two sides of this right triangle, how do you get the third side AD?
ladder: After dropping the altitude, triangle ABD has a right angle at D - so what special name does the longest side, AB, have here?

The Pythagoras theorem says: in a right triangle, hypotenuse squared equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides. With hypotenuse AB = 2a and one leg BD = a, what is AB squared, and what is BD squared?
If AB squared is the leg BD squared plus the leg AD squared, how do you rearrange that to get AD squared on its own?
Once AD squared = (2a)^2 - a^2 = 4a^2 - a^2, what is AD itself?
refreshers.answer: The Pythagoras theorem is the Class 9 rule for right triangles: the square of the hypotenuse (the side across from the 90-degree corner) equals the sum of the squares of the two legs. So in any right triangle, leg^2 + leg^2 = hypotenuse^2, and you can rearrange it to find any one side from the other two: a missing leg^2 = hypotenuse^2 - other leg^2. Here, in right triangle ABD, the hypotenuse is AB = 2a and one leg is BD = a, so AD^2 = AB^2 - BD^2 = (2a)^2 - a^2 = 4a^2 - a^2 = 3a^2, which gives AD = a times the square root of 3 (we take the positive root since a length is positive). That third side, AD = a*sqrt(3), is exactly what lets us read off sin, cos and tan of 30 and 60 degrees from this triangle.
refreshers.returnCue: Now that AD = a*sqrt(3) is in hand, we have all three sides - a, a*sqrt(3), 2a - and can read off the 30 and 60 degree ratios.

nodes.title: Trigonometric ratios of 0 and 90 degrees
nodes.brief: State the limiting values of the trigonometric ratios at 0 and 90 degrees, including which are undefined.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The limiting argument at 0 degrees.
Imagine angle A in a right triangle shrinking toward 0 degrees. The opposite side BC shrinks to zero while the hypotenuse AC approaches AB. In the limit: sin 0 = 0/AC = 0, cos 0 = AB/AC = 1, tan 0 = 0/AB = 0. Since cosec 0 = 1/sin 0 = 1/0, cosec 0 is undefined; cot 0 = cos 0/sin 0 is also undefined.

PART 2 — The limiting argument at 90 degrees.
As angle A grows toward 90 degrees, the opposite side BC approaches the hypotenuse AC and the adjacent side AB shrinks to zero. In the limit: sin 90 = 1, cos 90 = 0, tan 90 = 1/0 so tan 90 is undefined. Also sec 90 = 1/cos 90 = 1/0 so sec 90 is undefined; cosec 90 = 1, cot 90 = 0.

PART 3 — The full table at 0 and 90 degrees.
Summary: sin 0 = 0, cos 0 = 1, tan 0 = 0, cosec 0 = undefined, sec 0 = 1, cot 0 = undefined; sin 90 = 1, cos 90 = 0, tan 90 = undefined, cosec 90 = 1, sec 90 = undefined, cot 90 = 0. Notice the complementary symmetry: the 0 degree values of sin match the 90 degree values of cos.
keyMoves: Use the degenerate-triangle limit to get sin 0 = 0 and cos 0 = 1
Identify cosec 0, cot 0, tan 90, sec 90 as undefined
Apply the complementary pattern between 0 and 90 degrees
misconceptions: Claiming tan 90 = 0 instead of undefined
Saying cos 0 = 0
Forgetting which boundary ratios are undefined

nodes.title: Evaluate expressions at specific angles
nodes.brief: Substitute the exact values of the specific angles into a numeric trigonometric expression and simplify.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Know the exact values to substitute.
From the standard table: sin 30 = 1/2, cos 30 = sqrt(3)/2, sin 45 = cos 45 = 1/sqrt(2), sin 60 = sqrt(3)/2, cos 60 = 1/2, tan 30 = 1/sqrt(3), tan 45 = 1, tan 60 = sqrt(3), sin 0 = 0, cos 0 = 1, sin 90 = 1, cos 90 = 0. Memorise these before attempting evaluation questions.

PART 2 — Substituting and computing.
For sin 60 cos 30 + sin 30 cos 60: substitute (sqrt(3)/2)(sqrt(3)/2) + (1/2)(1/2) = 3/4 + 1/4 = 1. For 2 tan^2 45 + cos^2 30 - sin^2 60: substitute 2*(1)^2 + (sqrt(3)/2)^2 - (sqrt(3)/2)^2 = 2 + 3/4 - 3/4 = 2.

PART 3 — Common pitfalls.
When you see sin^2 30, it means (sin 30)^2 = (1/2)^2 = 1/4, not sin(30^2). Always square the ratio value, not the angle. After substitution, reduce all fractions to a common denominator before adding. Check: every trig ratio value at a standard angle is one of {0, 1/2, 1/sqrt(2), sqrt(3)/2, 1, 1/sqrt(3), sqrt(3), 2, 2/sqrt(3), undefined}.
keyMoves: Look up each specific-angle ratio's exact value
Substitute carefully, squaring where indicated
Combine fractions to a single simplified number
misconceptions: Substituting the wrong angle's value
Forgetting to square in sin^2 or tan^2 terms
Arithmetic slips when adding the fractions

nodes.title: Apply the identity sin^2 A + cos^2 A = 1
nodes.brief: Derive and apply the fundamental identity sin^2 A + cos^2 A = 1 from the Pythagoras theorem.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Deriving the identity from Pythagoras.
In right triangle ABC right-angled at B: AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 (Pythagoras). Divide both sides by AC^2: (AB/AC)^2 + (BC/AC)^2 = 1. Since AB/AC = cos A and BC/AC = sin A, we get cos^2 A + sin^2 A = 1, valid for all acute angles A (0 <= A <= 90).

PART 2 — Using the identity to find a missing ratio.
If sin A = 3/5, then cos^2 A = 1 - sin^2 A = 1 - 9/25 = 16/25, so cos A = 4/5 (positive for acute A). Similarly if cos A = 5/13, then sin^2 A = 1 - 25/169 = 144/169, so sin A = 12/13. This saves constructing a full triangle.

PART 3 — Rearrangements for proofs.
The identity cos^2 A + sin^2 A = 1 can be rearranged as sin^2 A = 1 - cos^2 A or cos^2 A = 1 - sin^2 A. In identity proofs, look for differences of squares like (1 - sin A)(1 + sin A) = 1 - sin^2 A = cos^2 A, which often lets you collapse a complicated numerator or denominator.
keyMoves: Start from AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 and divide by AC^2
Recognise the resulting squares as cos^2 A and sin^2 A
Use the identity to convert between sin and cos
misconceptions: Writing cos^2 A + sin^2 A = 0
Confusing this with the sec/tan identity
Dropping the square when applying it

refreshers.trigger: States or divides by AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 labelled (Pythagoras) as a known fact without re-explaining the Pythagoras theorem
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: Quick one before we go on: this whole derivation starts from AB squared plus BC squared equals AC squared, called Pythagoras - what does that statement actually say?
ladder: In a right triangle, the two short sides meeting at the 90-degree corner are the legs and the long side across from it is the hypotenuse. Which one is biggest?
The Pythagoras theorem links them: if you build a square on each leg, how do their two areas compare to the square built on the hypotenuse?
So if the legs are 3 and 4, what is 3 squared plus 4 squared, and what does that make the hypotenuse?
Here the legs are AB and BC and the hypotenuse is AC - so what equation does Pythagoras give you for them?
refreshers.answer: The Pythagoras theorem is a result from Class 9 geometry about right triangles: the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides (the legs). In symbols, for right triangle ABC right-angled at B, the legs are AB and BC and the hypotenuse is AC, so AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2. It is always true for a right triangle - that is why we can write it down as a known fact and not re-prove it. A quick check: legs 3 and 4 give 3^2 + 4^2 = 9 + 16 = 25 = 5^2, so the hypotenuse is 5. In this node that equation AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 is the starting line of the whole derivation - we divide every term by AC^2 and the two leg-over-hypotenuse pieces turn into cos A and sin A, giving sin^2 A + cos^2 A = 1.
refreshers.returnCue: Good - with AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 in hand as our known fact, let us divide through by AC^2 and watch the identity appear.

nodes.title: Apply the identity 1 + tan^2 A = sec^2 A
nodes.brief: Derive and apply the identity 1 + tan^2 A = sec^2 A and its cot/cosec counterpart.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Deriving 1 + tan^2 A = sec^2 A.
Start from AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 and divide both sides by AB^2: 1 + (BC/AB)^2 = (AC/AB)^2. Since BC/AB = tan A and AC/AB = sec A, we get 1 + tan^2 A = sec^2 A, valid for 0 <= A < 90 degrees (A = 90 excluded because sec 90 is undefined).

PART 2 — Deriving cot^2 A + 1 = cosec^2 A.
Now divide both sides of AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 by BC^2: (AB/BC)^2 + 1 = (AC/BC)^2. Since AB/BC = cot A and AC/BC = cosec A, we get cot^2 A + 1 = cosec^2 A, valid for 0 < A <= 90 degrees (A = 0 excluded because cosec 0 is undefined).

PART 3 — Rearrangements used in proofs.
From sec^2 A - tan^2 A = 1, you can factor the left side as (sec A - tan A)(sec A + tan A) = 1, meaning sec A - tan A = 1/(sec A + tan A). Similarly cosec^2 A - cot^2 A = 1. These factored forms appear frequently in identity proofs. Example: sec^2 45 - tan^2 45 = 2 - 1 = 1. Correct.
keyMoves: Divide the Pythagoras relation by AB^2 to get 1 + tan^2 A = sec^2 A
Rearrange to sec^2 A - tan^2 A = 1 when needed
Use the parallel identity cot^2 A + 1 = cosec^2 A
misconceptions: Writing 1 + sec^2 A = tan^2 A
Forgetting the angle restriction A < 90 degrees (sec undefined at 90)
Mixing up the tan and cot versions

nodes.title: Prove and simplify trigonometric identities
nodes.brief: Prove a trigonometric identity by transforming one side using the reciprocal, quotient, and Pythagorean identities until it equals the other.
nodes.quote: Prove that sec A (1 - sin A)(sec A + tan A) = 1.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Strategy: work on one side only.
To prove an identity, pick the more complicated side and transform it using known identities until it equals the other side. Never move terms across the equals sign as if solving an equation — that assumes the result, which is circular. Use reciprocal definitions, tan A = sin A/cos A, and the Pythagorean identities as your tools.

PART 2 — Worked example: prove sec A (1 - sin A)(sec A + tan A) = 1.
Replace sec A = 1/cos A and tan A = sin A/cos A: (1/cos A)(1 - sin A)(1/cos A + sin A/cos A) = (1/cos A)(1 - sin A)((1 + sin A)/cos A) = (1 - sin A)(1 + sin A)/cos^2 A = (1 - sin^2 A)/cos^2 A = cos^2 A/cos^2 A = 1. Done.

PART 3 — Checklist for identity proofs.
Step 1: convert all ratios to sin and cos (or use sec/tan directly). Step 2: look for a Pythagorean substitution (1 - sin^2 A = cos^2 A, sec^2 A - 1 = tan^2 A, etc.). Step 3: factor differences of squares (a - b)(a + b) = a^2 - b^2 when you see conjugate pairs. Step 4: cancel common factors in numerator and denominator. If stuck, try multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate of a binomial expression.
keyMoves: Convert all ratios to sin and cos (or use sec/tan and cot/cosec identities directly)
Apply sin^2 A + cos^2 A = 1 or 1 + tan^2 A = sec^2 A to cancel terms
Simplify one side until it equals the target side
misconceptions: Manipulating both sides as if solving an equation rather than proving
Forgetting to substitute a Pythagorean identity to finish
Cancelling incorrectly across sums

narrative: The chapter builds trigonometry from a single right triangle outward. It first defines the three primary ratios sin, cos, tan as ratios of sides of a right triangle, then the three reciprocal ratios cosec, sec, cot, and the bridging relation tan A = sin A / cos A. Because the ratios depend only on the angle (a similar-triangles argument), knowing one ratio fixes all the others — taught through Example 1 (given tan A, find the rest). The chapter then derives exact ratios for the specific angles 45°, 30°, 60° from special triangles and 0°/90° from a limiting argument, tabulating them in Table 8.1 so learners can evaluate concrete expressions (Exercise 8.2). Finally it uses the Pythagorean relation AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 to derive the three identities (cos^2 A + sin^2 A = 1, 1 + tan^2 A = sec^2 A, cot^2 A + 1 = cosec^2 A), which culminate in the terminal skill: proving and simplifying trigonometric identities (Examples 9-12, Exercise 8.3).
absentNotes: This rationalised edition stays within acute-angle, right-triangle trigonometry. It omits: the unit-circle / coordinate definition of the ratios and angles beyond 90°; radian measure; graphs of trigonometric functions; sum/difference and double-angle formulas as derived results (sin(A+B), cos(A+B) appear only as objects to disprove or as given data, never derived); inverse trigonometric functions; the law of sines and law of cosines for general triangles; and any heights-and-distances / angle-of-elevation application chapter (treated separately, not here). Proofs of the identities are given only for the right-triangle acute-angle case, not for general angles.
